titleStmt.title: Kuwait 2022 – Speaker 13 – Conversation (Xrenig) 1.6 – Racism; Dialects
WIBARAB Corpus
titleStmt.funder: Funded by the European Research Council under the Grant Agreement 101020127

publicationStmt.distributor: Austrian Center for Digital Humanities and Cultural Heritage

recording.p: Recorded during Kuwait Fieldwork Campaign Gunda Kinzl 2022

prefixDef.p: Private URIs using the teiCorpusHeader prefix are pointers to any element in the WIBARAB teiCorpus document.
Private URIs using the share prefix are pointers to audio file residing on the WIBARAB network share.
Private URIs using the geo prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a place element in VICAV Geodata list.
Private URIs using the dmp prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB DMP document..
Private URIs using the zotid prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a biblStruct element in the TEI export of the VICAV Zotero Group library.
Private URIs using the sources prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB list of sources.

spanGrp.span: After the war, after the 1980s, racism got worse here
And tribal intolerance increased a lot here
Intolerance
It wasn't found before the war
It wasn't found before
After the war there was a lot of racism, before we were Shi'ite and Sunni living together, the Sedentary folk and the Bedouins lived together, but after the war the situation was the opposite
We don't know
No, now it's...
The subject of Shi'itism spread ((or the question of the racism against the Shi'ites?)), the subject of tribalism spread, the preservation of the dialects increased, to prove who I am

A small child of the ʕAǧmān would come to you, and tell you "How are you? Is everything good? And how is your mother? And how is..." (the woman mimicks the heavy dialect of the ʕAǧmān characterised by the -š suffix for the 2.p.sg.f.)
You would talk like this because you want people to recognise that you are from the ʕAǧmān

A woman from the Dawāsīr would come to you (and say) "How are you? How are you doing? Is everything fine?" (the woman mimicks the heavy dialect of the Dawāsīr characterised by the -ć / -s suffix for the 2.p.sg.f.) This is so you can notice that she is from the Dawāsīr
And so, this generation in the 1980s started to protect their language, their speech, their dialect
So that others can notice that I am from the ʕAǧmān, from the Dawāsīr, from the ʕAwāzim
In many languages, dialects, people would take care as to how their pronounce the words, so that others can notice (for example) that I am from the ʕAwāzim
Tell us how the ʕAwāzim would speak...
For example, "Reem, you didn't eat lunch, you didn't eat your lunch today, Reem. Where did you go yesterday?" /She is mimicking the dialect of the ʕAwāzim/

This is the ʕAwāzim, and then a woman of the ʕAǧmān would come to you, "Reem what is your lunch for today? What did they tell you? How are you doing? Did you go to the doctor, and what did he tell you? " /She is mimicking the dialect of the ʕAǧmān/
The ʕAǧmān would always speak with the š
The purpose of this does not go back to the ʕAǧmān but rather to the Maḥāfīḏ̣, who are the purest clan of the ʕAǧmān, and their speech is like how I am talking to you
They are the most native tribe of the ʕAǧmān, the Maḥāfīḏ̣
The inhabitants of Abu Xlēfa are counted as natives, the inhabitants of Mangar are counted as natives, and the inhabitants of Finṭās are counted as natives
A clan of the ʕAǧmān which is the Maḥāfīḏ̣
But the rest, unfortunately, are the ones who mimick their words
The ʕAǧmān, the ʕAǧmān, look they are my neighbours, the Maḥāfīḏ̣, and loads of the ʕAǧmān, they are originally from the Ṣabāḥiyya district
I was living with them for a long time and they never pronounces the š sound

But other ʕAǧmān came in, and they were the ones who took up the practice of pronouncing the letter as such, and most of them go back to, she is angry at me /referring to another woman/, to Niǧrān in Saudi Arabia ((should I put here the note that Reem tells me - that the Naǧrānis are considered to be low class because they have no Naǧdi connection##))
They are the ones who pronounce the letter š
"How are you? Are you fine? Is everything ok? How are you? How is your family?" /speaker mimicks the speech of the ʕAǧmān/ Do you understand?

It is very famous now to do this and it has spread with the children, for example, I have girls, my daughter Reem goes to school ((this doesnt make sense, she is called Reem, then her daughter is called sara, but why does she use her name here??))
Sara, I tell her "Sara, how is everything at school?" She says "I am scared of my friends who are of the ʕAwāzim tribe"
I told her "Why?" She said "If I don't tell them that I am from the ʕAwāzim (tribe), they do not speak to me, they don't like me." See how there is racism amongst children.
You are young sweetheart, she says "No, I told them I am Sara from the ʕAwāzim"
She is scared of them, and she loves the girls
Yes, Maryam who is in the fourth year of primary school ((not sure what rabʕ is here, whether it's fourth or not#))
In front of the teachers, they asked her, because she is white and cute and blonde, "What are you?"
She looked at her friends and saw that they are all from the ʕAnaza, and she said "I am from the ʕAnaza"
The reason being that she could not say the actual name of her tribe, they would hate her
Because they would tell you, tribal racism after the war became plentiful
It became very strong
They would alienate you
Especially in the area of Aṣ-Ṣabāḥiyya
If you excuse me, I always thought that racism was always between the Kuwaitis and the foreigners
No, no, not at all. It was between the Kuwaitis and the same tribe and #
From the same tribe, you see me, (for example) I am friends with other people outside the tribe and I won't befriend people within my same tribe
For example my friend, you see, she would be a Lebanese, an Egyptian, my neighbour would be as such
The tribes harbour loads and loads of racism
But, it is tribal, for example even if my neighbours are from the Muṭayr, if my neighbours are from the ʕAǧrān, from the ʕAwāzim
I have a neighbour here,may God protect her, she lives with the ʕAwāzim, but she is from the Muṭayr, she loves them and she says "I send them gifts, I bring them food, but they don't want me"
I said "Maybe it's because you are from the Muṭayr." (She said) "Really? I didn't even think about this." I said "They are ʕAwāzim, they don't want anyone unless they are also ʕAwāzim"
I will tell you about my work, my place of work, I will give you a living example ((or current example?))
There is a lot of racism at my place of work, a lot of racism between Shi'ite and Sunna and between the tribes
Where is your actual place of work?
My place of work is at the governorate of the general party
Yes, at the governorate of the general party, but for the Kuwaitis not for the foreigners
I have Shi'ite co-workers, you see girls working with us who are Shi'ite, to the extent that one day a Shi'ite inspector came to us (check translation##)
He brought some halva, because he got a raise or something like that, and he brought faṭāyir and sweets and things like that
And he sent it with the receptionist to the girls working inside

They were there (and they asked) "What is this?" (The receptionist said) "This is from the head of department, Abū Yūsif, he got sweets and.." (They said) "Take it away, he is Shi'ite, we don't eat the same food as the Shi'ite"
Yes, it is so! Do you see before...
When I went to university, to AUK, the first thing that they thaught me was to respect the tribes and how to accept others
And this thing, I only learned it when I entered AUK
Before that, because I was raised in Aṣ-Ṣabāḥiyya, the effect of the tribal environment was influencing me very much
But when I got to AUK I experienced variety and I experienced culture, and there were multiple times where we learned to break away from tribalism and racism
Do you want to drink tea or coffee?
And from there onwards I started to change and to learn how to accept others
Tea, you don't want tea?
And I no longer held on to the racism, after 7 years of schooling I changed
Will you have breakfast with us today?
